title: Request for modification to podman-restart service to support restart=unless-stopped in addition to restart=always
body:
### Feature request description

There is no discounting the value of the podman-restart service in pursuance of effectuating a proper restart of containers subsequent to a reboot which have the "restart=always" condition set. However, there are times when it is desirous of always having a container restart on reboot but only restart (in a functioning environment) on unless-stopped. Adding the option for having podman-restart work in this condition would offer great utility.

The most flexible instantiation of such a modification (limited to being just based on restart=whatever) would allow the configuration of any of the three possible cases by the person configuring podman-restart.

1) Function when restart=always
2) Function when restart=unless-stopped
3) Function when restart=always or restart=unless-stopped

By the way, the ultimate modification would be for podman-restart to be able to restart a container based on any set of criteria that could be ascertained via "podman inspect". Why not?

Stuart, N3GWG

comments.body: We already support a `unless-stopped` restart policy, but I recall that it was implemented before `podman-restart.service` and as such was not fully functional. What are the issues with is, exactly - does it not restart after reboot at all, or does it always restart after reboot, even when the user manually stopped the container?

Here is the use case I am encountering:

You boot up Fedora CoreOS (in my case under ProxMox) succeeded by firing up a container with restart=unless-stopped (I do this via portainer). All is good and if the container crashes it restarts automatically.

If you reboot Fedora CoreOS (via say a "sudo reboot") then Fedora CoreOS will stop the containers started as "restart=always" but anything else is just left to be crashed on reboot. I would think the ExecStop should stop all containers it can find to exact the most orderly shutdown.

Since originally posting this response I engaged in some greater review of how things work and this is what I ended up doing to solve the issue:

I have modified some initialization scripts so that upon initial boot-up and execution of cloud-init within ProxMox it now does a number of things like "systemctl enable --now podman-restart.service" and as well I have modified the systemd file so it will now start-up containers that are in a 'created' or 'exited' state as well as those with restart=always or restart=unless-stopped configured. For the purposes of my environment, this customization (along with stopping all containers on ExecStop) works best.

`
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman $LOGGING start --all --filter restart-policy=always,restart-policy=unless-stopped
`
`
ExecStart=/usr/bin/podman $LOGGING start --all --filter status=exited,status=created
`
`
ExecStop=/bin/sh -c '/usr/bin/podman $LOGGING stop $(/usr/bin/podman container ls --filter restart-policy=always,restart-policy=unless-stopped -q)'
`


Stuart, N3GWG

AFAIK docker does not restart containers with unless-stopped after boot. Their docs states that the container is not started if the docker deamon is restarted. https://docs.docker.com/config/containers/start-containers-automatically/

So I don't think this is something the podman-restart unit should do. If you want this you can always create your own unit for it.

Paul,

> AFAIK docker does not restart containers with unless-stopped after boot. Their docs states that the container is not started if the docker deamon is restarted. https://docs.docker.com/config/containers/start-containers-automatically/
> 

You seem to be conflating docker and podman. I am not using docker, I am using podman. Can you disambiguate as to what you mean to say?

> So I don't think this is something the podman-restart unit should do. If you want this you can always create your own unit for it.

You can see the code for the modifications I made to my environment instantiated herein above. It does seem to be working for me. I had not fully understood I could make those changes at first or exactly how in a Fedora CoreOS environment (as it appeared read-only to me at first).

Stuart, N3GWG

A reboot counts as stopped. We try to be docker compatible, and AFAIK docker does not restart the containers after boot. If you want this just use the always policy. unless-stopped is the same as always except that it does not restart after boot.

So I say this is functioning exactly as designed and if we change it there will be zero difference between unless-stopped and always which makes no sense.

Paul,

> A reboot counts as stopped. We try to be docker compatible, and AFAIK docker does not restart the containers after boot. If you want this just use the always policy. unless-stopped is the same as always except that it does not restart after boot.

Perhaps that is true in terms of reboots, but read my comments as follow hereupon.

> So I say this is functioning exactly as designed and if we change it there will be zero difference between unless-stopped and always which makes no sense.

This makes no sense to me. It is my understanding (and do correct me if I am wrong) that if I use restart=unless-stopped and I manually stop a container it will not restart until I restart it. However, if I have restart=always and I stop the container, it will restart anyway (even if I do not manually restart it and there is no interceding reboot). If so, then there is a difference betwixt the two beyond what you state.

Stuart, N3GWG

If you manually stop a container with podman stop it will not be restarted with always. Otherwise it would be impossible to ever stop a container with always policy.
Looking at the doc: https://docs.podman.io/en/latest/markdown/podman-create.1.html#restart-policy
It says they are identical for podman.

Paul,

Okay, now I understand what you meant, I did not realize that. In that case it makes sense to just use always and I then agree that modification of the podman-restart configuration is of little value. However, it was interesting to learn how it's configuration works now.

Stuart, N3GWG
